Causal Encoding Generative Modeling (CausalEGM)

CausalEGM is a deep learning framework for nonlinear dimension reduction and generative modeling of the dependency among covariate features affecting treatment and response. The core insight is that of those thousands of covariates, only a small structured subset actually matters causally — and that subset can be learned. CausalEGM decomposes the latent space into three disjoint parts: confounders that affect both treatment and outcome, instrument-like features that affect treatment only, and prognostic features that affect outcome only. This structured decomposition allows CausalEGM to identify a minimal latent covariate feature subvector that affects both treatment and outcome, enabling accurate estimation of treatment effects even in high-dimensional settings.

Overview

All the models we’ve covered so far (TARNet, CFRNet, CEVAE) work well when the confounder space is low-dimensional and tractable. Real observational data — genomics, EHR records, fMRI scans — has thousands of covariates x. Directly conditioning on all of them causes the curse of dimensionality: propensity scores become unreliable, matching breaks down, and neural networks overfit.

CausalEGM is a deep learning framework for nonlinear dimension reduction and generative modeling of the dependency among covariate features affecting treatment and response. The core insight is that of those thousands of covariates, only a small structured subset actually matters causally — and that subset can be learned.

The key idea: dependency-aware latent decomposition

The key idea is to partition the latent feature vector into different independent components that play different roles in the generative models for treatment and outcome. This partitioning allows identifying a minimal latent covariate feature subvector that affects both treatment and outcome.

Concretely, CausalEGM decomposes the latent space z into three disjoint parts:

z = [ z_c | z_t | z_y ]

Component Role
z_c Confounders — affect both treatment and outcome
z_t Instrument-like — affect treatment only
z_y Prognostic — affect outcome only, not treatment

This is the structural insight that separates CausalEGM from CEVAE. CEVAE infers a single unstructured z; CausalEGM forces z to be causally structured from the start.

How CausalEGM worksh

  1. The bidirectional encoder-decoder

CausalEGM establishes a bidirectional transformation between the high-dimensional confounders space and a low-dimensional latent space where the density is known (e.g., multivariate normal distribution). Unlike a standard VAE which only goes x → z, CausalEGM learns both directions: f: x → z (encoder) and g: z → x̂ (decoder/generator). This cycle consistency means no information is lost — you can reconstruct x from z, proving the compression is lossless and faithful.

  1. The structured latent decomposition — the real innovation

CausalEGM simultaneously learns to embed the high-dimensional covariates into a low-dimensional latent space, then build generative models for treatment given latent features and for outcome given treatment and latent features. The key idea is to partition the latent feature vector into different independent components that play different roles.

The partition is: - z_c (confounders) → fed into both the treatment model and outcome models. These are the variables you must adjust for. - z_t (instruments) → fed into the treatment model only. Like instrumental variables in econometrics — they shift treatment but have no direct effect on outcomes. - z_y (prognostic) → fed into the outcome models only. They predict outcomes regardless of treatment but don’t cause selection bias.

This structure is enforced via an adversarial mutual information penalty that drives z_c ⊥ z_t ⊥ z_y — the three components are statistically independent of each other.

  1. The generative models on top of z

With the latent space structured, CausalEGM builds: - A treatment model p(t | z_c, z_t) — analogous to a propensity score, but in compressed latent space - Two outcome heads H₀(z_c, z_y) and H₁(z_c, z_y) — conditioned only on the causally relevant subset of z, not the full thing

For continuous treatments, this generalises cleanly to a dose-response function H(t, z_c, z_y) → ŷ(t) where t ∈ ℝ. This is something binary-only models (TARNet, CFRNet, CEVAE) cannot do at all.

  1. Joint training objective

Everything is trained end-to-end by minimising four losses simultaneously:

Loss Purpose
𝓛_recon Reconstruction — ‖x − g(f(x))‖² ensures z encodes all of x
𝓛_treatment Factual treatment prediction from z_c, z_t
𝓛_outcome Factual outcome prediction from z_c, z_y
𝓛_KL + 𝓛_indep Gaussian prior on z + enforces z_c ⊥ z_t ⊥ z_y
  1. Why this beats prior methods

The improvement is substantial when the sample size is large and the covariate is of high dimension. The key advantages over the models we’ve already seen are:

  • vs CEVAE: CEVAE infers a single unstructured latent z — it doesn’t tell you which parts of z are confounders vs instruments vs prognostic factors. CausalEGM’s partition is the causal structure itself, not just a compression.
  • vs TARNet/CFRNet: Those operate directly in the high-dimensional x space with no compression. MMD balancing in 1000-dimensional space is statistically unreliable.
  • vs classical SDR: SDR-based causal inference approaches consider only linear dimension reduction, which limits applicability. CausalEGM captures nonlinear relationships.
  • Continuous treatment: None of the previous four architectures handle t ∈ ℝ natively. CausalEGM directly estimates the full dose–response curve E[Y(t)] for any t.

The one known limitation

The joint use of an encoder and a generative decoder introduces a structural loop — such circularity violates the acyclicity assumption fundamental to Bayesian networks and causal diagrams. CausalEGM also relies on deterministic functions to establish the mapping between observed data and latent features, which can limit the model’s ability to capture and quantify uncertainty. This motivated the successor model CausalBGM (2025), which replaces the encoder entirely with a fully Bayesian posterior, restoring a clean DAG structure and adding proper uncertainty quantification.

Packages and function availability

packages <- c(
  "tidyverse",
  "RCausalML",
  "torch",
  "causaldata",
  "ForCausality",
  "mlbench",
  "gridExtra"
)

cat("Installed packages:\n")
Installed packages:
print(sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE))
Warning: replacing previous import 'stats::decompose' by 'igraph::decompose'
when loading 'RCausalML'
Warning: replacing previous import 'stats::spectrum' by 'igraph::spectrum' when
loading 'RCausalML'
   tidyverse    RCausalML        torch   causaldata ForCausality      mlbench 
        TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE         TRUE 
   gridExtra 
        TRUE 
invisible(lapply(packages, function(pkg) {
  suppressPackageStartupMessages(library(pkg, character.only = TRUE))
}))

find_project_root <- function(start = getwd()) {
  cur <- normalizePath(start, winslash = "/", mustWork = TRUE)
  repeat {
    if (file.exists(file.path(cur, "DESCRIPTION"))) return(cur)
    parent <- dirname(cur)
    if (identical(parent, cur)) return(NULL)
    cur <- parent
  }
}

causal_egm_fn <- NULL
if (exists("causal_egm", where = asNamespace("RCausalML"), mode = "function")) {
  causal_egm_fn <- getFromNamespace("causal_egm", "RCausalML")
} else {
  root <- find_project_root()
  if (!is.null(root) && requireNamespace("pkgload", quietly = TRUE)) {
    pkgload::load_all(root, quiet = TRUE, export_all = FALSE)
  }

  if (exists("causal_egm", where = asNamespace("RCausalML"), mode = "function")) {
    causal_egm_fn <- getFromNamespace("causal_egm", "RCausalML")
  } else if (exists("causal_egm", mode = "function")) {
    causal_egm_fn <- get("causal_egm", mode = "function")
  } else {
    stop(
      paste(
        "causal_egm() is unavailable in installed RCausalML and local source fallback failed.",
        "Install/update RCausalML or install pkgload to enable local source loading."
      )
    )
  }
}
Warning: replacing previous import 'stats::decompose' by 'igraph::decompose'
when loading 'RCausalML'
Warning: replacing previous import 'stats::spectrum' by 'igraph::spectrum' when
loading 'RCausalML'
Using device: cpu
Using device: cpu

Global setup and hyperparameters

run_fast   <- TRUE
device_use <- NULL

if (requireNamespace("torch", quietly = TRUE)) {
  device_use <- if (torch::cuda_is_available()) "cuda" else "cpu"
}

set.seed(42)
if (requireNamespace("torch", quietly = TRUE)) torch::torch_manual_seed(42)

BATCH_SIZE    <- if (run_fast) 512L  else 256L
N_EPOCHS      <- if (run_fast)  50L  else 150L
LR            <- 1e-3
LR_DISC       <- 5e-4
WEIGHT_DECAY  <- 1e-4
HIDDEN_DIM    <- if (run_fast)  64L  else 128L
DIM_C         <- if (run_fast)   4L  else   8L
DIM_T         <- if (run_fast)   2L  else   4L
DIM_Y         <- if (run_fast)   2L  else   4L
LAMBDA_RECON  <- 1.0
LAMBDA_TREAT  <- 2.0
LAMBDA_OUTCOME <- 2.0
LAMBDA_DISENT <- 0.5
MAX_GRAD_NORM <- 1.0

Load IHDP data (with synthetic fallback)

base_url <- "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data"
cols     <- c("treatment", "y_factual", "y_cfactual", "mu0", "mu1",
              paste0("X", 0:24))

chunks <- vector("list", 9L)
for (i in seq_len(9L)) {
  url <- sprintf("%s/ihdp_npci_%d.csv", base_url, i)
  tmp <- tryCatch(read.csv(url, header = FALSE), error = function(e) NULL)
  if (!is.null(tmp) && nrow(tmp) > 0L) {
    colnames(tmp) <- cols[seq_len(ncol(tmp))]
    chunks[[i]]   <- tmp
  }
}
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_1.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_2.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_3.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_4.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_5.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_6.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_7.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_8.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
Warning in file(file, "rt"): URL
'https://raw.githubusercontent.com/uber/causalml/master/docs/examples/data/ihdp_npci_9.csv':
status was 'Failure when receiving data from the peer'
df <- do.call(rbind, Filter(Negate(is.null), chunks))

if (!is.null(df) && nrow(df) > 0L) {
  replications <- if (run_fast) 2L else 10L
  df           <- df[rep(seq_len(nrow(df)), replications), ]
  rownames(df) <- NULL
  message("Loaded IHDP (all 9 files x ", replications,
          " replications): ", nrow(df), " x ", ncol(df))
} else {
  df <- NULL
}

if (is.null(df) || nrow(df) == 0L) {
  d <- tryCatch(
    synthetic_data(mode = 1, n = 5000, p = 25, sigma = 1.0, adj = 0),
    error = function(e) synthetic_data(mode = 1, n = 5000, p = 25, sigma = 1.0)
  )
  df           <- as.data.frame(d$X)
  colnames(df) <- paste0("X", 0:(ncol(df) - 1))
  df$treatment  <- d$w
  df$y_factual  <- d$y
  df$y_cfactual <- ifelse(d$w == 1, d$b - 0.5 * d$tau, d$b + 0.5 * d$tau)
  df$mu0        <- d$b - 0.5 * d$tau
  df$mu1        <- d$b + 0.5 * d$tau
  message("Using synthetic fallback: ", nrow(df), " x ", ncol(df))
}
Using synthetic fallback: 5000 x 30

Build matrices and split train/test

binfeats <- 7:25
contfeats <- 1:6
perm      <- c(binfeats, contfeats)
xcols     <- paste0("X", 0:24)

X          <- as.matrix(df[, xcols][, perm])
treatment  <- as.integer(df$treatment)
y          <- as.numeric(df$y_factual)
y_cf       <- as.numeric(df$y_cfactual)
mu0        <- as.numeric(df$mu0)
mu1        <- as.numeric(df$mu1)

cat("Rows:", nrow(X), "| Covariates:", ncol(X), "\n")
Rows: 5000 | Covariates: 25 
cat("Treatment prevalence:", round(mean(treatment), 3), "\n")
Treatment prevalence: 0.522 
n         <- nrow(X)
set.seed(42)
idx       <- sample.int(n)
train_n   <- floor(0.8 * n)
train_idx <- idx[seq_len(train_n)]
test_idx  <- idx[(train_n + 1L):n]

X_train         <- X[train_idx, , drop = FALSE]
treatment_train <- treatment[train_idx]
y_train         <- y[train_idx]
mu0_train       <- mu0[train_idx]
mu1_train       <- mu1[train_idx]

X_test         <- X[test_idx, , drop = FALSE]
treatment_test <- treatment[test_idx]
y_test         <- y[test_idx]
mu0_test       <- mu0[test_idx]
mu1_test       <- mu1[test_idx]

cat("Train size:", nrow(X_train), "| Test size:", nrow(X_test), "\n")
Train size: 4000 | Test size: 1000

Feature scaling

preprocess_features <- function(train_x, test_x) {
  cont_idx <- 20:25

  means <- colMeans(train_x[, cont_idx, drop = FALSE])
  sds   <- apply(train_x[, cont_idx, drop = FALSE], 2L, sd)
  sds[sds == 0] <- 1.0

  scale_cols <- function(mat) {
    sweep(sweep(mat[, cont_idx, drop = FALSE], 2L, means, "-"), 2L, sds, "/")
  }

  scaled_train             <- train_x
  scaled_test              <- test_x
  scaled_train[, cont_idx] <- scale_cols(train_x)
  scaled_test[, cont_idx]  <- scale_cols(test_x)

  list(train_x = scaled_train, test_x = scaled_test,
       means = means, sds = sds)
}

proc           <- preprocess_features(X_train, X_test)
X_train_scaled <- proc$train_x
X_test_scaled  <- proc$test_x

Train CausalEGM

causalegm_fit <- causal_egm_fn(
  X                  = X_train_scaled,
  treatment          = treatment_train,
  y                  = y_train,
  dim_c              = DIM_C,
  dim_t              = DIM_T,
  dim_y              = DIM_Y,
  hidden_dim         = HIDDEN_DIM,
  num_epochs         = N_EPOCHS,
  batch_size         = BATCH_SIZE,
  learning_rate      = LR,
  learning_rate_disc = LR_DISC,
  weight_decay       = WEIGHT_DECAY,
  lambda_recon       = LAMBDA_RECON,
  lambda_treat       = LAMBDA_TREAT,
  lambda_outcome     = LAMBDA_OUTCOME,
  lambda_disent      = LAMBDA_DISENT,
  max_grad_norm      = MAX_GRAD_NORM,
  verbose            = !run_fast,
  device             = device_use
)

history <- causalegm_fit$history
cat("CausalEGM model fitted.\n")
CausalEGM model fitted.

Evaluate treatment effects

evaluate_causalegm <- function(model, test_x, mu0_true, mu1_true) {
  mu0_pred <- as.numeric(predict(model, test_x, type = "mu0"))
  mu1_pred <- as.numeric(predict(model, test_x, type = "mu1"))
  ite_pred <- tryCatch(
    as.numeric(predict(model, test_x, type = "ite")),
    error = function(e) mu1_pred - mu0_pred
  )

  ite_true <- mu1_true - mu0_true
  pehe     <- sqrt(mean((ite_pred - ite_true)^2))
  ate_true <- mean(ite_true)
  ate_pred <- mean(ite_pred)
  eps_ate  <- abs(ate_pred - ate_true)
  rmse_y0  <- sqrt(mean((mu0_pred - mu0_true)^2))
  rmse_y1  <- sqrt(mean((mu1_pred - mu1_true)^2))

  cat(strrep("-", 45), "\n")
  cat(sprintf("True ATE      : %.4f\n", ate_true))
  cat(sprintf("Predicted ATE : %.4f\n", ate_pred))
  cat(sprintf("eps_ATE       : %.4f\n", eps_ate))
  cat(sprintf("sqrt(PEHE)    : %.4f\n", pehe))
  cat(sprintf("RMSE Y(0)     : %.4f\n", rmse_y0))
  cat(sprintf("RMSE Y(1)     : %.4f\n", rmse_y1))
  cat(strrep("-", 45), "\n")

  list(
    pehe     = pehe,     eps_ate  = eps_ate,
    ite_true = ite_true, ite_pred = ite_pred,
    mu0_pred = mu0_pred, mu1_pred = mu1_pred,
    ate_true = ate_true, ate_pred = ate_pred,
    rmse_y0  = rmse_y0,  rmse_y1  = rmse_y1
  )
}

eval_res <- evaluate_causalegm(causalegm_fit, X_test_scaled, mu0_test, mu1_test)
--------------------------------------------- 
True ATE      : 0.4984
Predicted ATE : 0.5566
eps_ATE       : 0.0581
sqrt(PEHE)    : 0.1182
RMSE Y(0)     : 0.2118
RMSE Y(1)     : 0.2235
---------------------------------------------

Visualize training and effect estimates

plot_results <- function(history, ite_true, ite_pred, model, train_x, train_t) {
  n_epochs <- length(history$train)
  hist_mat <- vapply(
    history$train,
    function(h) c(h$loss, h$recon, h$treat, h$outcome, h$disent),
    numeric(5L)
  )

  hist_df <- tibble::tibble(
    epoch   = seq_len(n_epochs),
    loss    = hist_mat[1L, ],
    recon   = hist_mat[2L, ],
    treat   = hist_mat[3L, ],
    outcome = hist_mat[4L, ],
    disent  = hist_mat[5L, ]
  )

  p_loss <- tidyr::pivot_longer(hist_df, -epoch,
                                names_to  = "metric",
                                values_to = "value") |>
    ggplot(aes(epoch, value, color = metric)) +
    geom_line(alpha = 0.9) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(title = "Training Loss Curves", x = "Epoch", y = "Loss")

  p_ite <- ggplot(
    tibble::tibble(ite_true = ite_true, ite_pred = ite_pred),
    aes(ite_true, ite_pred)
  ) +
    geom_point(alpha = 0.45, color = "steelblue") +
    geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "red") +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(
      title = sprintf("ITE Scatter (sqrt(PEHE) = %.3f)",
                      sqrt(mean((ite_pred - ite_true)^2))),
      x = "True ITE", y = "Predicted ITE"
    )

  ps   <- as.numeric(predict(model, train_x, type = "propensity"))
  p_ps <- ggplot(
    tibble::tibble(ps = ps, treat = as.factor(train_t)),
    aes(ps, fill = treat)
  ) +
    geom_histogram(position = "identity", bins = 30L, alpha = 0.5) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c("0" = "steelblue", "1" = "coral"),
      labels = c("Control", "Treated")
    ) +
    theme_minimal() +
    labs(title = "Learned Propensity Scores",
         x = "Propensity score", y = "Count", fill = NULL)

  gridExtra::grid.arrange(p_loss, p_ite, p_ps, nrow = 1L)
}

plot_results(
  history    = history,
  ite_true   = eval_res$ite_true,
  ite_pred   = eval_res$ite_pred,
  model      = causalegm_fit,
  train_x    = X_train_scaled,
  train_t    = treatment_train
)

Final metrics

cat(strrep("=", 60), "\n")
============================================================ 
cat("CausalEGM Results\n")
CausalEGM Results
cat(strrep("=", 60), "\n")
============================================================ 
cat(sprintf("True ATE       : %.4f\n", eval_res$ate_true))
True ATE       : 0.4984
cat(sprintf("Predicted ATE  : %.4f\n", eval_res$ate_pred))
Predicted ATE  : 0.5566
cat(sprintf("ATE Error      : %.4f\n", eval_res$eps_ate))
ATE Error      : 0.0581
cat(sprintf("PEHE           : %.4f\n", eval_res$pehe))
PEHE           : 0.1182
cat(sprintf("RMSE Y(0)      : %.4f\n", eval_res$rmse_y0))
RMSE Y(0)      : 0.2118
cat(sprintf("RMSE Y(1)      : %.4f\n", eval_res$rmse_y1))
RMSE Y(1)      : 0.2235
cat(strrep("=", 60), "\n")
============================================================

Summary and conclusion

This tutorial converts the Python CausalEGM notebook into an R-first workflow using RCausalML. The same conceptual functions are preserved: IHDP loading, preprocessing, model fitting, treatment-effect estimation, and visualization. The final model estimates both potential outcomes and individual treatment effects, and reports benchmark metrics such as ATE error and PEHE.

Resources

  • IHDP files used in CausalML examples
  • CausalML documentation
  • RCausalML repository
  • Counterfactual Inference and Learning Systems
